role_summary: Southern Christian Leadership Conference Director of Direct Action (1962-1968); trained by James Lawson at the Nashville workshops; strategist of the May 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade (the decision to send school-age children into the streets, which dramatically intensified the campaign's media impact); director of the 1965 Selma voting rights campaign; co-initiator (with Martin Luther King Jr.) of the 1968 Poor People's Campaign.
ai_reading: Bevel is referenced twice in the catalog (in the SNCC-Nashville-organizers cohort and in the Freeman Hrabowski (entry 45) bio where his strategy as the architect of the 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade is the structural condition that put Hrabowski in jail at age twelve). He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee. His standalone catalog page anchors the Lawson Nashville-workshop alumni cohort alongside LaFayette, Lewis, Nash, Barry, Vivian, and others; it also surfaces the catalog's clearest documented case of a strategic-decision-shaping-an-interviewee's-life-arc, the Children's Crusade design choice that produced the Hrabowski biographical anchor. The novel observation his page surfaces is the strategy-credit distribution problem: Bevel originated the Children's Crusade tactic; King decided to deploy it; the historiography credits both in roughly equal measure; the embedding's references to the May 1963 Birmingham campaign in the corpus's catalog pages run primarily through King and the campaign-as-a-whole rather than through Bevel by name.
biographical_paragraph: Bevel was born October 19, 1936 in Itta Bena, Mississippi. He enlisted in the U.S. Navy as a teenager, served four years, and on discharge enrolled at American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville in 1959, where he joined James Lawson's Tuesday-night nonviolence workshops alongside Bernard LaFayette, John Lewis, Diane Nash, and others. He participated in the 1961 Freedom Rides and married Diane Nash in 1961. In 1962 he joined the Southern Christian Leadership Conference as Director of Direct Action, the strategist role he would hold through 1968. In May 1963 he proposed the tactical decision that became the Birmingham Children's Crusade: sending school-age Black children into the streets in waves so that the campaign could continue past the point where adult arrests had exhausted the Birmingham movement. The strategy's success (the Connor fire-hose and police-dog response, then-Vice-President Lyndon Johnson's pressure on John F. Kennedy to introduce civil rights legislation) is documented in Adam Fairclough's To Redeem the Soul of America (University of Georgia Press, 1987), the canonical scholarly history of SCLC. He directed the 1965 Selma voting rights campaign and co-initiated the 1968 Poor People's Campaign with King. After King's 1968 assassination Bevel's organizational positions became increasingly heterodox; he ran briefly for the U.S. Presidency in 1992 on Lyndon LaRouche's ticket and later served as Louis Farrakhan's Nation of Islam minister in Chicago. He died December 19, 2008 in Springfield, Virginia at seventy-two.

sources.title: Adam Fairclough, To Redeem the Soul of America: The Southern Christian Leadership Conference and Martin Luther King, Jr.
sources.publisher: University of Georgia Press, 1987; WorldCat OCLC 14933060
